# <Pattern name>

Author:
Created:
Last reviewed:
Next review due:
Review scope: sources, red flags, scope boundary, comprehension

## What this page is

Define the observable pattern in plain language and state who wrote the page.

Add the pattern alignment visual immediately after this section when a visual is
necessary for beginner comprehension.

## What this page is not

This page does not diagnose the reader, prove that a posture is harmful, or
provide individualized care. It does not promise to correct the pattern or
explain all pain.

## Red flags: when to stop reading and seek care

Inline the pattern-relevant red flags, cite them, and link to
`../about/red-flags.md` before any self-management themes.

## Plain-language overview

Start with the reader's pain point: the common observations, search phrases, or
training questions that bring beginners to this page.

Then give the working definition, neutral comparison, normal-variation note, and
two to four "how to notice this in yourself" observations. Label every
self-observation method as observation, not diagnosis.

## What mainstream sources generally agree on

Name the pattern's likely movement contributors. Use mechanism-level language:
which muscle groups, what control problem, what daily-position load, and why
that contributor matters. Cite each contributor.

## What is uncertain or mixed

Name contested or uncertain evidence plainly. Include at least one skeptical or
critical source when available. This section protects the page from turning an
observable pattern into a body-is-broken story.

## Commonly recommended self-management themes

Describe general themes, not a corrective routine. Use the reader journey:

> user's pain point -> observable pattern -> likely movement contributors -> exercises that train the missing options

Recommended internal order:

1. Reduce anxiety / reframe.
2. Clear red flags first.
3. Motor learning or pattern awareness.
4. Strength category A.
5. Strength category B, when relevant.
6. Mobility options, with an "option, not proof" caveat.
7. General training context.

For each linked exercise, use this inline annotation:

```md
- **[Exercise name](../exercises/exercise-slug.md)**
  - *Fix reason:* one sentence on why this exercise maps to this pattern's mechanism.
  - *Used muscles:* primary muscles first; secondary muscles second.
  - *Important note:* one key cue, common mistake, or safety caveat.
  - ![Optional thumbnail alt text](../media/movements/exercise-slug-sequence.png)
```

Only link exercises that exist as repository files. Omit the thumbnail line
unless the referenced image exists.

## What to avoid

Name unsafe interpretations and scope boundaries. Include pattern-specific
warnings against posture-shaming, correction promises, and generic routines
when pain, nerve symptoms, medical history, or major movement limits change the
situation.

## When to see a professional

Distinguish professional types: physical therapist for persistent symptoms,
medical clinician for red flags or health concerns, and strength coach for
technique or performance questions without pain or medical context.

## Where to next in this primer

List three to five next-step links, including at least one principle page and
one anchor exercise page from the menu.

## Sources

Add at least five distinct named sources, including at least two institutional,
clinical, or public-health sources and at least one peer-reviewed paper when
the pattern has safety or pain-adjacent claims.

## Author and review date

Add author and review date with credential disclosure.
